test_id_8.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_9.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_9.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is the payer of last resort and the pharmacy must first bill Medicare and any other liable coverage before billing Medicaid. The provider manual adds that Medicare and Medicaid payments, if any, must be accepted as payment in full, and that full-benefit dual eligibles must use their Medicare Part D plan first for Part D drugs.
answers.policy_definition:
Pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer before billing NYS Medicaid, and this includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit. The policy is broader than the manual because it does not specifically name Medicare Part D or full-benefit dual eligibles. It still supports the first-bill-the-primary-payer rule.

NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable liable third parties before billing Medicaid, and Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Providers must exhaust existing benefits and coordinate with the proper primary payer before submitting to NYS Medicaid. The policy is less specific because it does not address the fraud warning or the exact adjudication sequence.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicare members who also have Medicaid must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find a covered Part D drug or use the Part D exception and appeals process.

Pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage before submitting to Medicaid. A claim that bypasses the responsible third-party payer is fraudulent, and Medicaid is paid only after that payer has adjudicated the claim. The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
similarities: Both sources require the pharmacy to bill the primary or liable coverage before Medicaid. They also treat Medicaid as secondary to other available coverage [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources support coordination of benefits before Medicaid payment is considered. The policy says providers must exhaust other benefits, and the manual says the pharmacy must submit to other coverage first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual is more specific for full-benefit dual eligibles because it says Medicare Part D must be used first for Part D drugs. The policy update states the broader primary-payer rule but does not specifically name Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual adds that Medicare and Medicaid payments, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. The policy update does not add that payment-in-full instruction in the cited passage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources require the pharmacy to bill the primary or liable coverage before Medicaid. They also treat Medicaid as secondary to other available coverage [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources support coordination of benefits before Medicaid payment is considered. The policy says providers must exhaust other benefits, and the manual says the pharmacy must submit to other coverage first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual is more specific for full-benefit dual eligibles because it says Medicare Part D must be used first for Part D drugs. The policy update states the broader primary-payer rule but does not specifically name Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual adds that Medicare and Medicaid payments, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. The policy update does not add that payment-in-full instruction in the cited passage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is

test_id_10.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_10.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must bill Medicare or another responsible third party first, and Medicaid pays only after those sources are used and only up to the Medicaid allowed amount.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid says that, after other coverage is billed first, it will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted coverable claims up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount. The policy statement is broader and does not specifically restate the Medicare payment cap or the Part B cost-sharing prohibition.

NYS Medicaid requires providers and pharmacies to bill applicable liable third parties before billing Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and pays only after other benefits are exhausted. The policy also says pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer, including the correct pharmacy or medical benefit pathway.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
The total Medicare and Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be recovered from dual-eligible individuals.

Pharmacies must submit claims to a members other coverage before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and a claim that bypasses responsible third-party payment is fraudulent billing. The pharmacy may resubmit to Medicaid only after the claim is properly adjudicated with the third-party liability payer.
similarities: Both sources say other liable coverage must be billed before Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources describe Medicaid as the payer of last resort for these claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about Medicare payment limits and the ban on recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader and focuses on coordination of benefits and patient responsibility up to the Medicaid allowed amount, rather than the Medicare-specific payment cap [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources say other liable coverage must be billed before Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources describe Medicaid as the payer of last resort for these claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual is more specific about Medicare payment limits and the ban on recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader and focuses on coordination of benefits and patient responsibility up to the Medicaid allowed amount, rather than the Medicare-specific payment cap [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_10.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_10.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_10.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual is more explicit and current on dual-eligible co-payment responsibility. For Medicare Part B, the pharmacy cannot seek to recover deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid dual-eligible individuals, and Medicare and Medicaid payment accepted by the pharmacy is payment in full.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update says dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules. It does not specifically address co-payment responsibility or pharmacy recovery of Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. That makes the policy source broader and less specific than the provider manual.

The Medicaid Update does not give a direct copay-waiver rule for dual-eligible members. It only says that duals are subject to Medicare rules, so it does not create a separate waiver standard. The provider manual is the more operationally specific source on this point.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare Part B benefits, a pharmacy provider cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. If Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made.

Providers cannot proactively waive copays. A copay may be waived only case by case based on financial need and at the members request.
similarities: Both sources place dual-eligible members within the Medicare and Medicaid framework. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7, New York State Medicaid Program:
differences: The provider manual gives a specific prohibition on recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dual-eligible individuals, while the Medicaid Update only gives a general statement that duals are subject to Medicare rules. [New York State Medicaid Program:
comparison: Both sources place dual-eligible members within the Medicare and Medicaid framework. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7, New York State Medicaid Program:

The provider manual gives a specific prohibition on recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dual-eligible individuals, while the Medicaid Update only gives a general statement that duals are subject to Medicare rules. [New York State Medicaid Program:
answers.caveats: The policy source in this comparison is partial for this topic, so it does not directly answer dual-eligible co-payment responsibility.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_12.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual says Medicare and Medicaid payment for dual-eligible members must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member. The policy evidence is broader and confirms that duals are subject to Medicare rules, but it does not state the no-collection rule as directly.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update says duals are subject to Medicare rules. That supports Medicare control over cost-sharing responsibility, but it does not specifically say that the pharmacy may not collect Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member. The policy statement is therefore broader and less specific.

Medicaid is the payer of last resort and must be billed after other sources, including Medicare, have been exhausted. That is the closest policy match for the billing-order issue, but it is broader than the pharmacy manual because it does not focus specifically on Part D drug exclusions. It still confirms that Medicare is billed first for dual-eligible members.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare Part B services, the pharmacy cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. This is the direct billing rule for member cost-sharing on Part B claims.

For dual-eligible members, Medicaid generally does not cover drugs paid under Medicare Part D except for Medicaid-reimbursable drugs that are excluded by law. Claims submitted to Medicaid are still subject to Medicaid rules. The manual also gives examples such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs.
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as the primary framework for dual-eligible member cost-sharing or billing order [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources place limits on when Medicaid can be used for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual states the no-collection rule directly for Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance, while the Medicaid Update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual is more specific about Part D drug exclusions and Medicaid-reimbursable exceptions, while the policy source speaks more generally about Medicaid being the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as the primary framework for dual-eligible member cost-sharing or billing order [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources place limits on when Medicaid can be used for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The provider manual states the no-collection rule directly for Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance, while the Medicaid Update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual is more specific about Part D drug exclusions and Medicaid-reimbursable exceptions, while the policy source speaks more generally about Medicaid being the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
answers.caveats: The policy source in this comparison is broader than the provider manual and does not itself spell out the pharmacy co-payment collection rule.

It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_13.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual says Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dually eligible individuals. The policy supports the broader rule that Medicare is billed first and Medicaid is payer of last resort, but it is less specific about member responsibility for that cost-sharing.
answers.policy_definition: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so Medicare must be billed before Medicaid. If Medicare covers the service, NYS Medicaid may reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by law. The policy is broader and does not specifically say that the pharmacy may not collect the cost-sharing from the member.
answers.provider_manual_definition: For Medicare Part B benefits, the pharmacy must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment as payment in full. The pharmacy cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals. If Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made.
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare first and Medicaid after other reimbursement sources have been exhausted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

Both sources address Medicare Part B cost-sharing in the dual-eligible context [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual is member-protective and says the pharmacy cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy is less specific and focuses on billing order and possible Medicaid reimbursement of Part B coinsurance and deductible, rather than on direct member responsibility [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare first and Medicaid after other reimbursement sources have been exhausted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

Both sources address Medicare Part B cost-sharing in the dual-eligible context [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The provider manual is member-protective and says the pharmacy cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy is less specific and focuses on billing order and possible Medicaid reimbursement of Part B coinsurance and deductible, rather than on direct member responsibility [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_14.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual says Medicaid does not require dual-eligible members to pay Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance to the pharmacy, because the Medicare and Medicaid payment is accepted as payment in full. The policy evidence is broader and less specific: it says duals are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid is payer of last resort, with Medicare billed first and Medicaid only paying certain cost-sharing or excluded-drug situations.
answers.policy_definition:
The update says duals are subject to Medicare rules, but it does not address pharmacy copayment collection directly. It is therefore broader and less specific than the provider manual on who may be charged at the pharmacy. It confirms the general Medicare framework for duals rather than a billing rule for Part B cost sharing.

The policy says Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so Medicare and other reimbursement sources must be billed before Medicaid. It also says Medicaid may reimburse Part B coinsurance and deductible for dually enrolled Medicare and Medicaid members to the extent permitted by law. That is a broader billing-order rule than the provider manuals pharmacy-specific Part D exception.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare Part B pharmacy benefits, the pharmacy cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals. Any Medicare and Medicaid payment is accepted as payment in full. The manual also states that the total payment will not exceed what the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient.

For Medicare Part D dual-eligible members, Medicaid should not be billed except for drugs Medicaid can cover when the drug class is excluded from Part D by law. All claims for dual-eligible members that are submitted to Medicaid remain subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization. This is the closest provider-manual guidance on when Medicaid may still pay after Medicare coverage rules apply.
similarities: Both sources treat dual-eligible members as operating under Medicare-linked rules before Medicaid pays. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program:
Both sources recognize that Medicaid payment for duals can be limited by other coverage or legal rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program:
differences: The provider manual gives a direct pharmacy rule that Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be collected from dual-eligible members, while the policy update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines;

The policy is broader because it focuses on payer order and allowable Medicaid reimbursement, while the provider manual gives the more specific pharmacy billing exception for Part D and excluded drugs. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1; New York State Medicaid Program:
comparison: Both sources treat dual-eligible members as operating under Medicare-linked rules before Medicaid pays. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program:
Both sources recognize that Medicaid payment for duals can be limited by other coverage or legal rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program:

The provider manual gives a direct pharmacy rule that Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be collected from dual-eligible members, while the policy update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines;

The policy is broader because it focuses on payer order and allowable Medicaid reimbursement, while the provider manual gives the more specific pharmacy billing exception for Part D and excluded drugs. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1; New York State Medicaid Program:
answers.caveats: The policy update cited here is not a pharmacy copayment instruction, so the comparison is necessarily partial on that point.

test_id_15.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The current provider manual says Medicaid does not cover Medicare Part D drugs for full-benefit dual eligibles, and dual-eligible members must use Medicare Part D for covered drugs. On co-payment responsibility, the provider manual is explicit that Medicare and Medicaid payment accepted together is payment in full, while the policy update is broader and mainly says duals are subject to Medicare rules and Medicaid is payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: The July 2025 Medicaid Update says duals are subject to Medicare rules. It does not specifically say who must pay Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. The policy is therefore broader and less specific on co-payment responsibility.
answers.provider_manual_definition: For Medicare Part B services, the Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. A pharmacy provider cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals. This is the clearest current rule in the provider manual for cost-sharing responsibility.
similarities: Both sources place dual-eligible members under the Medicare-first framework for covered Medicare services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct no-balance-billing rule for Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance, while the Medicaid Update only states that duals are subject to Medicare rules and does not assign the cost-sharing obligation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
comparison: Both sources place dual-eligible members under the Medicare-first framework for covered Medicare services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual gives a direct no-balance-billing rule for Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance, while the Medicaid Update only states that duals are subject to Medicare rules and does not assign the cost-sharing obligation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
answers.caveats: The policy update chunk is partial for this topic, so it supports the Medicare-first framework but does not directly address copayment liability.

Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

On co-payment responsibility, the provider manual is explicit that Medicare and Medicaid payment accepted together is payment in full, while the policy update is broader and mainly says duals are subject to Medicare rules and Medicaid is payer of last resort.", "aligned_pairs": [{"provider_manual": "For Medicare Part B services, the Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. A pharmacy provider cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals. This is the clearest current rule in the provider manual for cost-sharing responsibility.", "policy_update": "The July 2025 Medicaid Update says duals are subject to Medicare rules. It does not specifically say who must pay Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. The policy is therefore broader and less specific on co-payment responsibility."}], "similarities": ["Both sources place dual-eligible members under the Medicare-first framework for covered Medicare services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7]."], "differences": ["The provider manual gives a direct no-balance-billing rule for Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance, while the Medicaid Update only states that duals are subject to Medicare rules and does not assign the cost-sharing obligation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7]."], "caveats": "The policy update chunk is partial for this topic, so it supports the Medicare-first framework but does not directly address copayment liability.", "policy_definition": [], "provider_manual_definition": []}
test_id_15.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_15.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_15.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs for Medicare Part D dual-eligible members, except for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list that are specifically excluded from Part D by law, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. The 2023 Medicaid Update is narrower and does not specifically address the Part D-excluded-drug rule.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update says some drugs that require administration by a practitioner may be billed by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. It also says pharmacy-billed practitioner administered drugs must be on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. This is broader guidance and does not specifically address Part D-excluded drugs.

The Medicaid Update explains billing for practitioner administered drugs, including pharmacy billing of certain drugs. It does not discuss Part D enrollment, formulary exceptions, or appeals. That makes it less specific to the dual-eligible Part D workflow.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid generally does not pay for prescription drugs for a Medicare Part D dual-eligible member. Medicaid only pays for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is specifically excluded from Part D by law, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. Claims for dual-eligible members are still subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

Full-benefit dual eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. If a needed drug is not covered, members and prescribers must use the Part D plans exception and appeals process. Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part D for these members.
similarities: Both sources connect Medicaid payment to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. The policy article is broader because it addresses practitioner administered drugs rather than Part D exclusions specifically [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct Part D rule for dual-eligible members and names the narrow drug categories Medicaid still covers, while the Medicaid Update only gives broader billing guidance for practitioner administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
comparison: Both sources connect Medicaid payment to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. The policy article is broader because it addresses practitioner administered drugs rather than Part D exclusions specifically [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

The provider manual gives a direct Part D rule for dual-eligible members and names the narrow drug categories Medicaid still covers, while the Medicaid Update only gives broader billing guidance for practitioner administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
answers.caveats: The Medicaid Update source does not directly discuss the Part D-excluded-drug rule, so the comparison is only partial.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.

It is not appropriate to bill

Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

test_id_16.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs for Medicare Part D members unless the drug is on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. The policy evidence is less specific on Part D-excluded drugs and mainly provides background on other Medicaid billing rules.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy update does not state a Part D exclusion rule in the same way. It instead says some practitioner administered drugs may be billed to Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy, and it points providers to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list for billing guidance. That makes it broader and less specific than the provider manual rule.

The policy update gives general Medicare billing direction. It says providers bill Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs and Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plan for other prescription drugs. If the member is not enrolled in Part D, Part D covered claims go to LINET to start enrollment and help payment.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid should not be billed for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless the drug meets the stated exception. Medicaid only pays for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D. Select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs are examples of that exception.

For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any drug class covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers should use the Part D plan formulary first. If needed, they should use the Part D exception and appeals process for prescriptions not listed on the plan formulary.
similarities: Both sources point providers to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list as part of deciding how a drug may be billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

Both sources treat Medicaid payment as limited by other coverage rules before Medicaid is used [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct Part D exclusion rule for Medicaid payment, while the policy update is only an indirect background source and does not specifically address Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

The provider manual says Medicaid may pay only for specific Part D-excluded drug classes, while the policy update focuses on general Medicare billing pathways such as Part B, Part D, MAPD, and LINET [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
