test_id_37.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_37.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: When required patient identifying information is missing, the pharmacist is generally not required to write it onto the prescription if the information is already available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. For a separate but related responsibility, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription and prescriber identity when the prescriber is unknown, and must consult the prescriber as needed on prescription changes.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update is broader and does not address missing patient identifiers directly. It says the pharmacy must use professional judgment for emergency department prescriptions when the assigned PCP is unavailable, and it must contact the PCP the next business day. That is only a partial match to the provider manual guidance on missing patient information.

The Medicaid Update says providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. That guidance is related, but it is broader and less specific than the provider manual. It does not use the same good-faith identity verification standard.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If the Medicaid members address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it on the prescription in certain situations. The pharmacist may rely on pharmacy records if the information is readily available and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill. The pharmacist may also rely on professional judgment if the prescription is otherwise believed to be legitimate.

If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. This is an affirmative verification duty, not just a general review step. The guidance does not add a separate follow-up timeline in this row.
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist or pharmacy to act when prescription information is incomplete or needs confirmation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources allow the pharmacist to rely on judgment or consultation rather than automatically rejecting the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual is specific about missing patient fields such as address, age, and CIN, while the Medicaid Update addresses emergency department fills and next-business-day PCP contact instead [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual requires a good-faith effort to verify an unknown prescriber’s identity, while the Medicaid Update only says to verify directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist or pharmacy to act when prescription information is incomplete or needs confirmation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources allow the pharmacist to rely on judgment or consultation rather than automatically rejecting the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual is specific about missing patient fields such as address, age, and CIN, while the Medicaid Update addresses emergency department fills and next-business-day PCP contact instead [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual requires a good-faith effort to verify an unknown prescriber’s identity, while the Medicaid Update only says to verify directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: If required patient information is missing, the pharmacist may not need to enter the missing address, age, or CIN if the information is already in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. For missing prescriber information, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription and the prescriber’s identity when the prescriber is unknown.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy does not directly address missing patient demographic or identifier information. It instead discusses professional judgment in emergency and restricted-recipient situations, including filling prescriptions and contacting the primary care provider. That makes it a broader and less specific match for this row.

The policy says providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. That is related, but it is broader than the manual’s specific rule about an unknown prescriber. It does not state the same good-faith verification duty.
answers.provider_manual_definition:

The pharmacist also is not required to enter it when the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. This is a limited exception for those specific patient data elements.

If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist is responsible for making a good-faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. The manual states this as an affirmative pharmacist responsibility. It applies when prescriber identification is uncertain.

test_id_38.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

test_id_39.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_39.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: If required patient identifying information is missing, the pharmacist generally does not have to enter it if the information is already available in the pharmacy records or the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. If the prescriber is unknown, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription and the prescriber’s identity.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy excerpt does not address missing patient identifiers directly. It instead requires professional judgment when filling prescriptions in an emergency department context, and it requires contact with the assigned PCP the next business day. That is related guidance, but it is broader and less specific.

Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. This is a broader verification rule than the manual’s unknown-prescriber standard. It supports prescriber contact, but it does not use the same good-faith identity-verification language.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it if the information is already in the pharmacy’s records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill. The pharmacist also is not required to enter it if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. This guidance applies to those specific patient identification fields.

When the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist is responsible for making a good-faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. The manual states this as an affirmative responsibility. It does not require a specific method in the excerpt.
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist to use judgment when prescription information needs clarification [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources treat prescriber contact or verification as part of the pharmacist’s duties when prescription information is incomplete or uncertain [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The manual gives a specific rule for missing patient address, age, or CIN, while the policy excerpt does not and instead discusses emergency department prescribing and next-business-day PCP contact [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The manual requires a good-faith effort to verify the prescriber’s identity when the prescriber is unknown, while the policy excerpt only says to verify directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist to use judgment when prescription information needs clarification [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources treat prescriber contact or verification as part of the pharmacist’s duties when prescription information is incomplete or uncertain [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The manual gives a specific rule for missing patient address, age, or CIN, while the policy excerpt does not and instead discusses emergency department prescribing and next-business-day PCP contact [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The manual requires a good-faith effort to verify the prescriber’s identity when the prescriber is unknown, while the policy excerpt only says to verify directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_40.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists are not required to add missing member address, age, or CIN to the prescription if the information is already available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. When the prescriber is unknown, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy does not give the same missing patient information rule. It instead says pharmacies should use professional judgment in certain emergency fills and contact the assigned PCP the next business day. That makes it broader and less specific than the provider manual rule.

The policy says providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. That is related, but it does not specifically require identity verification when the prescriber is unknown. It is broader and less specific than the provider manual guidance.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist does not have to enter it on the prescription in certain situations. The pharmacist may rely on pharmacy records if the information is already available and the requester is known. The pharmacist may also refrain from adding it if the prescription is otherwise believed to be legitimate.

If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity. The rule is about identification and validity checking before or during dispensing. It is a direct responsibility of the pharmacist.
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist or provider to use judgment and to verify prescription-related information before dispensing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources allow contacting the prescriber when needed, although the policy frames this as consulting on changes rather than identity verification [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing member address, age, or CIN, while the policy does not address that missing-information issue directly and instead discusses emergency fills [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual requires a good-faith effort to verify the prescriber’s identity when the prescriber is unknown, while the policy only says to verify directions for use and consult the prescriber on changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist or provider to use judgment and to verify prescription-related information before dispensing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources allow contacting the prescriber when needed, although the policy frames this as consulting on changes rather than identity verification [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing member address, age, or CIN, while the policy does not address that missing-information issue directly and instead discusses emergency fills [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual requires a good-faith effort to verify the prescriber’s identity when the prescriber is unknown, while the policy only says to verify directions for use and consult the prescriber on changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
answers.caveats: The policy source is only a partial match for the missing patient information issue, so the provider manual is the controlling source for that part of the comparison.

The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

test_id_41.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used for weight loss, and the newest Pharmacy Manual also says compounded products may not be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. The policy materials are consistent with that exclusion, though the manual’s compound rule is narrower and specifically addresses compounded claims.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid says drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable under SSA §1927(d)(2). The policy language is broader because it states the general coverage exclusion, not just the compounding limitation.

NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. It also lists drugs for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain as not reimbursable under the general drug coverage rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Compounds may not be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications under SSA §1927(d)(2), including drugs to treat weight loss. Ingredients in a compound claim must still meet the usual reimbursement and compendia support rules.

Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. The manual lists drugs used for treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain as examples of non-reimbursable drugs or drug uses.
similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
Both sources tie the exclusion to SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The Pharmacy Manual frames the rule in a compounding context and says compounds may not be made for excluded indications, including weight loss drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The Medicaid Update states the broader reimbursement exclusion for weight loss drugs and does not limit the statement to compounded products [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
Both sources tie the exclusion to SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The Pharmacy Manual frames the rule in a compounding context and says compounds may not be made for excluded indications, including weight loss drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The Medicaid Update states the broader reimbursement exclusion for weight loss drugs and does not limit the statement to compounded products [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.

supported for use.

a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_42.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used for weight loss or weight gain from reimbursement, and the newer provider manual also says compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications such as weight loss. The provider manual adds a compound-specific restriction.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain under SSA §1927(d)(2). The policy states the general drug exclusion and is broader than the compound-specific manual language.

NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. Drugs for weight loss or weight gain are specifically listed as not reimbursable.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. Ingredients in a compound claim must also be on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable Drugs and be supported for the intended route and indication.

Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the NYS List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for weight loss or weight gain are listed as non-reimbursable examples.

Both sources tie the exclusion to the Medicaid reimbursable drug framework rather than a separate special approval process [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it applies the exclusion to compounds and adds rules about excluded ingredients and intended indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it states the general non-reimbursable drug categories without the compound-specific restrictions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the state List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are listed as not reimbursable. The policy statement is the same, but it is broader because it lists the exclusion as an example set.

Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. The policy also names drugs to treat weight loss as excluded. The match is strong because both sources address the same compound limitation.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the NYS List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable. This is the current manual guidance for fee-for-service drug coverage.

Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. The manual specifically names drugs to treat weight loss as an excluded indication. This rule applies to compounded products and their reimbursement eligibility.
similarities: Both sources state that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also say compounded products may not be made for excluded indications such as drugs to treat weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is the newer and more current source, so it is the better anchor for current guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is more general in form, while the provider manual gives the same exclusion in the compound claim context and ties it to current manual instructions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources state that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also say compounded products may not be made for excluded indications such as drugs to treat weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
The provider manual is the newer and more current source, so it is the better anchor for current guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is more general in form, while the provider manual gives the same exclusion in the compound claim context and ties it to current manual instructions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_43.ground_truth:
"The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications."

test_id_44.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_44.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used to treat weight loss, and the provider manual also says compounded products may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications such as weight loss. The current provider manual is the most up-to-date source, so this is a current exclusion with a specific compounded-drug restriction as well.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid says drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable. The policy statement is broader because it lists the excluded drug use directly and does not focus on compounded products. It still points to the same exclusion category.

NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and drugs for weight loss are listed as not reimbursable. The policy update states the same coverage rule in a general way. It does not add the manuals compounded-drug limitation.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications under Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2), including drugs to treat weight loss. This means compounded prescriptions cannot be used to work around that exclusion. The manual also lists sexual dysfunction and cosmetic purposes as other examples.

Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and drugs used for weight loss are listed as not reimbursable. The manual ties the exclusion to the covered-drug list and to Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2). This is current guidance in the Pharmacy Manual.
similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources rely on the Medicaid excluded-drug framework in Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual adds a compound-specific rule that excluded drugs and excluded indications, including weight loss, cannot be used in compounded claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
The policy update states the exclusion more generally and does not separately discuss compounded products [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources rely on the Medicaid excluded-drug framework in Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual adds a compound-specific rule that excluded drugs and excluded indications, including weight loss, cannot be used in compounded claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
The policy update states the exclusion more generally and does not separately discuss compounded products [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

test_id_45.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_45.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes. The current provider manual says drugs used to treat weight loss are excluded from Medicaid reimbursement, and it also says compounds cannot contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications such as weight loss. The policy update is consistent and lists drugs for weight loss or weight gain as not reimbursable.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This policy statement is broader than the provider manual because it presents the same exclusion as a coverage limitation. It confirms that weight loss drugs are not reimbursable.

The policy update gives the same compound rule and is slightly more specific about examples. It says compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes. The policy is consistent with the manual on weight loss use.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid reimbursement excludes drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This is current pharmacy manual guidance for reimbursable drugs. The rule applies to the drug use itself, not only to a specific brand or product type.

Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications. The manual specifically names drugs to treat weight loss as an excluded indication. Refills and ingredient reimbursement still must follow the compound claim rules.
similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also block compounded products when the excluded indication is weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is newer and adds current compound claim details, while the policy update states the exclusion more generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The policy update lists additional excluded examples, such as sexual dysfunction and cosmetic use, while the provider manual frames the rule around excluded drugs and excluded indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

test_id_46.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs at the 340B acquisition cost when the claim is properly submitted as a 340B purchased drug claim. Current policy also requires claim-level 340B identifiers, and for NCPDP 340B claims the ingredient cost must be reported as 340B acquisition cost with the U&C field populated as applicable.
answers.policy_definition: 340B drugs must be identified on NYS Medicaid claims, and providers must report the NDC, actual acquisition cost, and units. For FFS claims, the UD modifier is required on the 340B drug line. This policy is broader than the manual because it explains submission requirements rather than the reimbursement formula.
answers.provider_manual_definition: For 340B purchased drugs, Medicaid reimburses at the 340B Acquisition Cost. A $10.18 professional dispensing fee applies if the drug qualifies and is not paid at U&C. The manual also says claims pay at U&C if that amount is lower than ingredient cost plus dispensing fee.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

test_id_46.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."

It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_47.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B acquisition cost when the claim is properly identified as a 340B purchased drug claim. NY Medicaid also requires claim-level 340B identifiers and reporting fields to support proper payment and avoid duplicate discounts.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy requires 340B drugs to be identified with the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid fee-for-service claims. Providers must also report the NDC and the actual acquisition cost in the claim. This is the claim submission framework that supports reimbursement, but it is less specific than the manual on the final payment amount.

The policy says 340B drugs must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on fee-for-service claims. It also requires the NDC and actual acquisition cost to be reported. That is the related operational rule, but it does not state the duplicate discount process as directly as the manual.

If a 340B drug claim is submitted with ingredient cost above the ceiling price, NY Medicaid returns denial code 708 and reject code 23. The pharmacy must resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost. This matches the manuals billing control concept, but the policy is more specific about the denial and correction process.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For 340B purchased drug claims, NY Medicaid reimburses at the 340B Acquisition Cost. The professional dispensing fee is $10.18 if it applies and the claim is not paid at usual and customary price. This is the current payment methodology for eligible 340B purchased drug claims.

NY Medicaid relies on claim-level 340B identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. The program does not use the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, and the identifiers are required on all 340B drug claims. Using the identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid removes the claim from rebate invoicing.

On 340B claims, the ingredient cost must be submitted as the 340B Acquisition Cost. The claim must also follow the required NCPDP field structure, including the usual and customary cost field. Using claim-level identifiers on non-340B claims is fraudulent billing.

differences: The provider manual states the actual reimbursement amount, while the policy focuses on the claim submission requirements for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual says NY Medicaid uses claim-level 340B identifiers instead of the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, while the policy only states the UD modifier and cost reporting rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The policy gives the specific denial codes for an ingredient cost above the ceiling price, while the provider manual only states that the ingredient cost must be submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format

appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.

New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
