provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

test_id_47.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_47.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_47.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_48.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed at 340B Acquisition Cost, not at a general drug-pricing benchmark. Current Pharmacy Manual guidance also requires claim-level 340B identifiers and the required pricing fields so the claim is recognized and paid correctly.
answers.policy_definition:
Drugs obtained through the 340B program must be identified with the UD modifier on New York State Medicaid fee-for-service claims. Providers must also report the National Drug Code, the number of units administered, and the actual acquisition cost in the charges field. This policy is broader than the manual because it describes claim reporting requirements rather than the reimbursement basis itself.

The update says 340B drugs on fee-for-service claims must use the UD modifier on the 340B drug line. It also says the National Drug Code must be reported. That is consistent with claim-level identification, but it is less specific than the manual about duplicate discount prevention.

The update requires the National Drug Code, units administered, and actual acquisition cost in the charges field for 340B reimbursement. It does not list the NCPDP coding fields, so it is a broader billing instruction than the manual.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For 340B purchased drugs, Medicaid reimburses at 340B Acquisition Cost. The usual dispensing fee of $10.18 applies if the claim is eligible and not paid at Usual and Customary pricing.

New York Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. It relies on claim-level 340B identifiers for all 340B drug claims to avoid duplicate discounts.

For 340B Medicaid claims in NCPDP format, the required fields include Submission Clarification Code 20, Basis of Cost Determination 08, ingredient cost as 340B Acquisition Cost, and Usual and Customary cost as the lowest net charge to cash customers.
similarities: Both sources require claim-level information to support 340B reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie 340B payment to actual acquisition cost rather than a generic billed amount [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The manual states the reimbursement basis directly as 340B Acquisition Cost, while the policy focuses on the UD modifier, National Drug Code reporting, and the charges field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual also specifies NCPDP pricing fields and the duplicate discount control process, while the policy is narrower and does not describe those fields [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
comparison: Both sources require claim-level information to support 340B reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie 340B payment to actual acquisition cost rather than a generic billed amount [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual states the reimbursement basis directly as 340B Acquisition Cost, while the policy focuses on the UD modifier, National Drug Code reporting, and the charges field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual also specifies NCPDP pricing fields and the duplicate discount control process, while the policy is narrower and does not describe those fields [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

The provider manual gives specific NCPDP fields, while the policy update only states that the NDC and actual acquisition cost must be reported [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

test_id_49.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_49.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_49.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_50.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, with a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 when applicable. NY Medicaid also requires the claim to be identified as 340B on submission so duplicate discounts are avoided.
answers.policy_definition:
340B drugs must be identified on the claim, and providers must report the NDC, actual acquisition cost, and units. NYS Medicaid will not reimburse if required NDC reporting is missing. This policy is broader on submission rules than on the exact payment rate.

340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. The claim must also include NDC and actual acquisition cost information. This is the closest policy match to the claim-level identification rule.

NCPDP 340B drug claims must be properly identified as 340B and submitted at 340B acquisition cost by invoice. The required fields for ingredient cost and U&C must be populated. This policy statement is less specific than the manual about the exact field codes.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
340B purchased drugs reimburse at the 340B Acquisition Cost. The professional dispensing fee is $10.18 when the drug meets covered outpatient drug requirements and is not paid at U&C. If U&C is lower, the claim pays at U&C.

NY Medicaid does not use the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File. It relies completely on 340B claim level identifiers to keep 340B claims from being invoiced for rebates twice. Those identifiers are required on the claim submission for all 340B drug claims.

For Medicaid 340B drug claims in NCPDP format, the claim must use SCC 20 and BCD 08. The ingredient cost must be submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost. The U&C field must also be reported correctly.
similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified at submission so Medicaid can process them correctly.
Both sources connect 340B payment to reported acquisition cost information.

Both sources require claim-level detail that supports avoiding duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual gives the current reimbursement rate and dispensing fee, while the policy update focuses more on reporting rules than payment methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual says NY Medicaid uses claim level identifiers instead of the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, while the policy update specifically names the UD modifier for FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual names the NCPDP field codes, while the policy update states the required cost and identification elements more generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

test_id_51.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, and services. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the pharmacy is responsible for billing timely to the correct party based on the applicable coverage pathway.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy source is narrower, but it shows that certain practitioner-administered drugs are covered when FDA-approved or supported by a medically accepted indication. It does not describe a bundled home infusion payment. It is therefore only a partial match to the home infusion coverage question.

The policy source confirms that pharmacy billing must be timely and sent to the appropriate party. It also says pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility must use the Pro-DUR process in NCPDP format. It does not state the full home infusion eligibility tests.

The policy source says the pharmacy is responsible for billing timely and to the appropriate party, including the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. It also requires Pro-DUR submission in NCPDP format when the pharmacy is not required to bill the facility. It is broader than the manual because it does not focus only on home infusion products.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, and services. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit.

Home infusion drugs can be covered under the pharmacy benefit only if they meet pharmacy-billing criteria. The drug must be billable in NCPDP format, deliverable to the place of administration, not included in a facility rate, and available from a Medicaid-enrolled dispensing pharmacy.

When a drug can be billed via NCPDP D.0, the pharmacy submits the claim as a pharmacy billing transaction. The manual also directs providers to separate medical-supply billing guidance when the product is not a pharmacy-billed item.
similarities: Both sources treat infusion-related drug coverage as dependent on the correct billing pathway rather than a single bundled home infusion rate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie payment to the proper claim submission route, including pharmacy billing and NCPDP-based processing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion drugs and supplies, while the policy update is narrower and addresses practitioner-administered drugs in the office setting [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives the home infusion coverage criteria for pharmacy-benefit billing, but the policy update only states general billing responsibility and does not list those criteria [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
comparison: Both sources treat infusion-related drug coverage as dependent on the correct billing pathway rather than a single bundled home infusion rate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie payment to the proper claim submission route, including pharmacy billing and NCPDP-based processing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual is specific to home infusion drugs and supplies, while the policy update is narrower and addresses practitioner-administered drugs in the office setting [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives the home infusion coverage criteria for pharmacy-benefit billing, but the policy update only states general billing responsibility and does not list those criteria [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
answers.caveats: The policy sources are partial matches, so the comparison relies most heavily on the current provider manual for home infusion guidance.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_52.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_52.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: NY Medicaid does not pay a bundled rate for home infusion. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed separately as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the policy source only supports separate drug billing more generally.
answers.policy_definition: Medicaid fee-for-service covers practitioner-administered drugs when they are FDA approved or otherwise supported by recognized compendia for a medically necessary covered service. The policy also notes that certain drugs and drug classes have separate billing guidance. This is broader than the home infusion rule and does not specifically address home infusion billing.
answers.provider_manual_definition: NY Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual also points to the Medicaid reimbursable drug list for pharmacy benefit billing.
similarities: Both sources support separate billing rules for covered drugs rather than a single bundled payment approach [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says the drugs and supplies must be billed through pharmacy or medical benefit pathways, while the policy update is broader and only addresses practitioner-administered drugs generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
comparison: Both sources support separate billing rules for covered drugs rather than a single bundled payment approach [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says the drugs and supplies must be billed through pharmacy or medical benefit pathways, while the policy update is broader and only addresses practitioner-administered drugs generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy chunk does not state who is responsible for billing home infusion specifically. It only gives broader guidance on coverage and billing for practitioner-administered drugs.

administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_54.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_54.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not pay a bundled rate for home infusion; home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed either as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The clearest billing responsibility in the retrieved evidence is that the pharmacy must bill timely and bill the appropriate party, although the policy source is broader than home infusion.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid fee-for-service covers certain drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office when FDA-approved or otherwise medically accepted indications are met. The policy also points providers to practitioner-administered drug billing guidance and clinical criteria. This is broader than home infusion and does not speak specifically to bundled home infusion billing.
answers.provider_manual_definition: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual also notes that reimbursable pharmacy drugs can be found on the Medicaid list of pharmacy benefits.
similarities: Both sources treat infused or administered drugs as separately governed Medicaid services rather than a single bundled payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy update is broader and addresses practitioner-administered drugs in an office setting [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
comparison: Both sources treat infused or administered drugs as separately governed Medicaid services rather than a single bundled payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy update is broader and addresses practitioner-administered drugs in an office setting [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy source does not specifically address home infusion billing responsibility, so the billing-responsibility point comes only from the provider manual and related pharmacy guidance.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled home-infusion payment. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the pharmacy must bill timely to the appropriate party.
answers.policy_definition:
Medicaid fee-for-service covers certain practitioner-administered drugs, including drugs given by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods, when the coverage criteria are met. This policy is broader than home infusion and focuses on covered practitioner-administered drugs rather than a bundled home-infusion rate.

The pharmacy is responsible for billing timely and to the appropriate party, including the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. This policy is broader than the home-infusion rule, but it supports the same operational point that the pharmacy carries the billing responsibility when it bills the drug.

Home infusion drugs may be covered through the pharmacy benefit if they meet pharmacy-benefit criteria. Those criteria include NCPDP billing format and direct delivery to the place of administration.

Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone is reimbursed under the pharmacy manual as a medication paid through NDC-based reimbursement methodology. If the pharmacy also bills for administration, that claim is separate and uses procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field.
answers.policy_definition: The policy chunk gives a broader drug-payment rule for vaccine cost for members 19 years of age and older. It says reimbursement is limited to no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy. It does not specifically address naloxone.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The pharmacy is reimbursed for the medication when it is billed via National Drug Code using reimbursement methodology. This is the current guidance for the drug claim. If the pharmacy also bills administration, that billing is separate.

comparison: Both sources address reimbursement for a drug product rather than only a service component [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

answers.caveats: The retrieved policy chunk is about vaccine reimbursement, so it does not directly match naloxone.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_56.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
test_id_56.pm_ground_truth: The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.
test_id_56.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

test_id_57.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone is reimbursed under the pharmacy manual’s normal drug-claim methodology when billed by NDC, with the medication paid through the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. If naloxone is also administered, the administration must be billed separately and is reimbursed at the current administration fee using procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI.
answers.policy_definition: The policy chunk is broader and does not specifically address naloxone or pharmacy drug billing. It says MMCPs reimburse an initial authorization of service for 60 days after notification from the HCBS provider. That is a general reimbursement rule, not a drug-claim payment rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The pharmacy is reimbursed for naloxone when the drug claim is billed via National Drug Code using reimbursement methodology. The ordering prescriber’s NPI must be entered in the Prescriber ID field. If administration is billed too, the claims must be submitted separately.
similarities: Both sources describe Medicaid payment as tied to a defined billing or authorization process, but the policy source is much broader than the pharmacy drug-claim rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
differences: The pharmacy manual gives a specific naloxone billing method using NDC and the ordering prescriber’s NPI, while the policy source only addresses general HCBS service reimbursement and does not mention naloxone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
comparison: Both sources describe Medicaid payment as tied to a defined billing or authorization process, but the policy source is much broader than the pharmacy drug-claim rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].

The pharmacy manual gives a specific naloxone billing method using NDC and the ordering prescriber’s NPI, while the policy source only addresses general HCBS service reimbursement and does not mention naloxone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
answers.caveats: The policy source is only a partial match, so it does not change the pharmacy manual’s naloxone billing instructions.

test_id_58.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
test_id_58.pm_ground_truth: The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.
test_id_58.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

answers.provider_manual_definition:
The pharmacy keeps these orders on file. If the drug and administration are both billed, the claims must be submitted separately, and the drug is reimbursed when billed by National Drug Code using reimbursement methodology.

Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or the patient’s designee before the item is prepared and submitted for payment. That is a compound-specific payment rule, not a naloxone-specific rule.

Multiple source drug reimbursement is only available for drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. That is the closest drug-coverage rule in the retrieved pharmacy manual, but it does not mention naloxone specifically.
similarities: Both sources use reimbursement language, but the policy chunk is about HCBS authorization rather than drug payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
differences: The pharmacy manual gives billing instructions for drug claims, including National Drug Code billing and separate administration billing under procedure code 96372, while the policy chunk gives a separate HCBS authorization rule and does not mention naloxone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
comparison: Both sources use reimbursement language, but the policy chunk is about HCBS authorization rather than drug payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].

The pharmacy manual gives billing instructions for drug claims, including National Drug Code billing and separate administration billing under procedure code 96372, while the policy chunk gives a separate HCBS authorization rule and does not mention naloxone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy chunks are not naloxone-specific, so the comparison is only a partial fallback from the approved row plan.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
